1: "Hear me, LORD, my plea is just (ṣeḏeq); listen to my cry. Hear my prayer — it does not rise from deceitful lips" — Christ as the only human being whose plea before God is perfectly just and completely without deceit. All human prayers are mixed with self-interest and incomplete honesty; Christ's prayer in John 17 ("I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do") is the only perfectly just petition. He is simultaneously the one who prays righteously (this verse) and the one whose intercession makes our imperfect prayers acceptable (Hebrews 7:25; Romans 8:34).
2: "Let my vindication come from you; may your eyes see what is right" — the prayer for divine vindication answered in the resurrection. The resurrection was the Father's verdict on the son's claim: the cross appeared to be defeat and judgment; the empty tomb declared him righteous. Romans 4:25: Christ "was delivered over to death for our sins and was raised to life for our justification." The vindication ("justification") of Christ is the ground of the justification of all who are in him.
3: "Though you probe my heart and examine me at night; though you test me, you will find that I have done nothing wrong; I have resolved that my mouth will not sin" — the examination that only Christ passed without finding sin. Hebrews 4:15: "we have one who has been tempted in every way, just as we are — yet he did not sin." 1 Peter 2:22: "He committed no sin, and no deceit was found in his mouth." The divine probing and testing of Christ found nothing — the verdict of perfect innocence that became the basis for the imputation of righteousness to all who believe (2 Corinthians 5:21).
4: "As for what others do — following your word I have kept myself from the ways of the violent" — Christ's separation from the patterns of the world. Though accused of being "a friend of tax collectors and sinners" (Matthew 11:19), Jesus remained without sin — he entered the company of the violent and corrupt not to participate but to rescue (Luke 19:10: "the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost"). His presence among sinners was not contamination but rescue — "he did not consider equality with God something to be used to his own advantage... he humbled himself" (Philippians 2:6-8) while remaining without sin.
5: "My steps have held to your paths; my feet have not stumbled" — the perfect walk of Christ. Every other human being has stumbled from the path (Psalms 14:3: "they have all turned away"); Christ alone walked the entire way without stumbling (Hebrews 12:2: "he endured the cross, scorning its shame"). The "path" language connects to Psalms 16:11 ("you make known to me the path of life") — the path of perfect obedience that Christ walked from Bethlehem to Calvary to the empty tomb, and which his people are enabled to walk in him (Colossians 2:6: "walk in him").
6: "I call on you, my God, for you will answer me; turn your ear to me and hear my prayer" — the confidence in answered prayer that characterized Christ's relationship with the Father. John 11:42: "I knew that you always hear me." John 17:1: "Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may glorify you." Christ prayed with the certainty of being heard — a certainty grounded in the unbroken communion of the eternal Father-Son relationship maintained through the incarnation. This confidence is extended to believers: "if we ask anything according to his will, he hears us" (1 John 5:14).
7: "Show me the wonders of your great love, you who save by your right hand those who take refuge in you from their foes" — the wonders of divine love reached their apex in the incarnation and crucifixion. John 3:16: "For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son." 1 John 4:9-10: "This is how God showed his love among us: He sent his one and only Son into the world... not that we loved God, but that he loved us and sent his Son as an atoning sacrifice for our sins." The "wonders of great love" (ḥasdêkā) are the covenant loyalties made visible in Christ.
8: "Keep me as the apple of your eye; hide me in the shadow of your wings" — Christ's intercession for his people. John 17:11: "Holy Father, protect them by the power of your name, those you have given me." John 17:15: "My prayer is not that you take them out of the world but that you protect them from the evil one." Christ prays the exact prayer of this verse on behalf of all believers — that they be kept as precious and sheltered as the apple of the divine eye. The prayer of the psalmist becomes Christ's prayer for the church.
9: "...from the wicked who are out to destroy me, from my mortal enemies who surround me" — the reality of deadly opposition that Christ faced. He was "surrounded" at the end: betrayed within his own circle, tried before the Sanhedrin, handed to Pilate, crucified between criminals, mocked by passersby. The "mortal enemies" were both human (the chief priests, the Roman soldiers) and cosmic (the powers and principalities — Colossians 2:15). Christ did not evade the surrounding — he allowed it to complete, trusting the Father to vindicate him.
10: "They close up their callous hearts, and their mouths speak with arrogance" — the hardness of heart that opposed and crucified Christ. John 12:40 (citing Isaiah 6:10): "He has blinded their eyes and hardened their hearts, so they can neither see with their eyes, nor understand with their hearts." The arrogance of the religious leaders who condemned Jesus ("if this man were a prophet, he would know..." Luke 7:39; "we have no king but Caesar" John 19:15) fulfilled this description completely.
11: "They have tracked down my steps, surrounding me; with eyes alert, to throw me to the ground" — the hunting of Christ in the Passion. John 18:3-8 describes the scene: Judas arriving with soldiers and officials carrying torches and weapons, surrounding Jesus in Gethsemane. Jesus stepped forward and said "I am he" — and they fell to the ground. The very "throwing to the ground" that the enemies intended for him happened to them at the sound of his divine self-identification. Even in his arrest, Christ demonstrated sovereign authority.
12: "They are like a lion hungry for prey, like a fierce lion crouching in cover" — the lion imagery connects to 1 Peter 5:8 (Satan as a roaring lion) and Revelation 5:5 (Christ as the Lion of Judah). The enemy crouches like a lion to attack the psalmist — ultimately, to attack Christ. But the attack on Christ became the instrument of the enemy's defeat: "having disarmed the powers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross" (Colossians 2:15). The lion that crouched to devour was disarmed by the willing sacrifice of the Lamb.
13: "Rise up, LORD, confront them, bring them down; with your sword rescue me from the wicked" — the prayer for divine intervention answered definitively in the resurrection and the final judgment. The risen Christ declares "I hold the keys of death and Hades" (Revelation 1:18) — the sword of judgment is now his. The final bringing-down of all who oppose Christ awaits the return: "The Lord Jesus will overthrow [the lawless one] with the breath of his mouth and destroy by the splendor of his coming" (2 Thessalonians 2:8). The prayer of the cross ("Father, into your hands I commit my spirit") is also a prayer for this confrontation.
14: "By your hand save me from such people, LORD, from those whose reward is in this life" — Christ's perspective on earthly reward. He consistently refused the earthly kingdom (John 6:15: "Jesus, knowing that they intended to come and make him king by force, withdrew again to a mountain by himself"). The temptation to pursue the kingdoms of the world (Matthew 4:8-10) was the temptation to "have his reward in this life." Christ's reward was deferred: "For the joy set before him he endured the cross" (Hebrews 12:2) — the reward was resurrection-life, not earthly power.
15: "As for me, I will see your face in righteousness; when I awake, I will be satisfied with seeing your likeness" — the culminating resurrection verse of this psalm range. "When I awake" (bĕhāqîṣ) is the "awaking" from death that the OT uses as a resurrection metaphor (Job 14:12; Isaiah 26:19; Daniel 12:2). This verse describes what Christ experienced at the resurrection: he "awoke" to see the Father's face in the righteousness he had maintained through death. For believers, this is the promise of final glory: 1 John 3:2 ("when Christ appears, we shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is") and Revelation 22:4 ("they will see his face").
